dialogue.utterance: Priority one dispatched for cardiac arrest. Unit 3 respond for a male with no pulse.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What's the reason you called 911 today?
The call is for a cardiac arrest at the patient's residence in Bon Air.
The patient is unresponsive. No eye opening observed.
No verbal response detected.
No motor response noted. Patient is completely unresponsive.
The airway is unsecured and non-maintainable. Jaw is not pliable.
No respirations observed. Confirming apneic status.
No pulse detected. Patient's skin is warm, not cold.
Blood pressure is unobtainable. Pulse is 0. Respirations are 0.
EKG reading shows no return of spontaneous circulation. Current state is asystole.
Glucose levels not measurable. No device access during assessment.
GCS is 3 (eye 1, verbal 1, motor 1). Patient is comatose.
Manual CPR initiated at 14:18 as per cardiac arrest protocol.
First King LT/I-gel airway insertion attempted. Size 5 selected but unsuccessful.
Autopulse CPR device deployed but failed. Suspected band issue.
Second King LT/I-gel airway attempted, size 4. Again, unsuccessful.
Oropharyngeal airway inserted successfully at 14:21 per standing order.
Vocal cords remain anterior and not visualized despite multiple attempts at intubation.
Oral intubation attempt at 14:26 failed. Suction performed at 14:27 for airway clearance.
Attempted intraosseous access at the left humeral head. Was unsuccessful.
Administered Epi 1:10,000 intraosseously at 14:25. No response noted.
Epi 1:10,000 administered again at 14:30 as per ALS protocol.
Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq administered at 14:33. Condition unchanged.
Epi 1:10,000 given at 14:35 as part of continued resuscitation.
Epi 1:10,000 administered again at 14:40 as per physician directive.
Monitored patient using 4-lead ECG starting at 14:39.
Patient repositioned at 14:18:30 during initial assessment.
Suction occurred at 14:27 to assist with oral airway insertion.
EKG at 14:40 shows asystole; rhythm unchanged.
Pulse remains undetectable at 14:40. No return of spontaneous circulation.
Continued manual CPR with no recovery of spontaneous circulation noted.
Followed direct cardiac arrest protocol number 2-7 without deviation.
Obtained official cease resuscitation order from Dr. Moll at 14:44.
Confirmed resuscitation cessation at 14:47. No further interventions were attempted.
Reassessments at 14:40 confirm vitals: pulse 0, respirations 0, BP 0/0, SpO2 0, asystole.
Verified access was intraosseous. Initial IO failed but multiple epinephrine doses administered.
Decision made to cease resuscitation. No transport requested.
Wife reported patient was alert and functioning reasonably normally at 11 AM.
According to wife, patient was out dancing the night before and seemed well.
Wife reported no allergies, chronic medications, or known health conditions.
Autopulse system failed the first attempt. No further re-use attempted due to failure.
Oropharyngeal airway was the most successful intervention accomplished by the rescue team.
King LT/I-gel insertion attempts were unsuccessful. Intubation also failed.
Patient found supine, apneic, no pulses. Resuscitation ceased with physician directive.
Wife has been advised of the outcome and agreed to no further transport.
Pain assessment not applicable. Patient is pulseless and unresponsive.
No localized or referred pain noted.
No provocation indicated during resuscitation.
No radiation of pain observed.
Severity not measurable. Patient is not breathing and pulseless.
Time of onset undetermined, however, no signs of pain at initial contact.
No symptoms reported beyond the cardiac arrest onset.
No allergies reported by spouse.
No medications identified during field assessment.
Pertinent past medical history: Neuro - Sleep Apnea.
Last seen alert and normal at 11 AM today according to patient's wife.
Events leading up to event: Patient went out dancing the previous evening, presented normally.
Patient is a 69-year-old male.
No petechiae, purpura, or bruises noted on skin at time of assessment.
Eyes open via reflex, Pupil size observed but no reactivity due to cardiac arrest.
No evident oral or nasal obstructions or secretions.
No breath sounds heard bilaterally during airway clearing attempts.
No abdominal rigidity or distention detected.
Visual inspection shows intact extremities with no visible deformities or wounds.
No rectal bleeding or urinary symptoms assessed.
Reconfirmed administration through IO route after initial failure at access.
IO access was attempted but not functional during first 4 epinephrine doses.
No pain response indicated due to cardiac arrest.
No signs of pain or discomfort detected.
Reassessed the sequence of calls. Patient was located in the bedroom, non-ambulatory.
Re-confirmed patient is not reporting any medications in home.
Reconfirmed past medical history of sleep apnea, reported by spouse.
Administered 15 LPM oxygen via BVM throughout initial resuscitation.
Adjusted patient's position per protocol at 14:18:30 for safe access.
Used suction intermittently to maintain oral airway access and clearing.
Oropharyngeal device replaced at 14:28 to ensure optimal airway patency.
Attempted recheck post-move to verify airway patency at 14:25.
Verified intraosseous access was not functional during anew attempts at 14:21.
All doses of epinephrine were administered via IO as per protocol for code.
Continued manual compression due to autopulse failure with consistent rhythm.
Checked EKG rhythm at multiple times, including 14:21, 14:26, and 14:40.
Monitored EKG via 4-lead at 14:39 in preparation for physician review.
All medication and CPR attempts documented per physician standing orders or calls.
Verified orders were from Chippenham and VCU before implementing cease of resuscitation.
Approximately 4 mg of epinephrine delivered +/- other support medications as dictated.
EKG at 14:40 remains asystole with no cardiac activity.
Verified vitals at 0 pulse, 0 respirations, BP unobtainable.
SpO2 remained unmeasurable due to no pulse at all checkpoints.
Patient's skin remained warm due to lack of rigor mortis for ~28 minutes.
Patient's family has been informed about the cessation of resuscitation at scene.
All care delivered included CPR, Epi, IO access, and EKG as per protocol.
No prior history of chest pain or any recorded cardiac episodes before this collapse.
Spouse acknowledges care process and agrees to reported conclusions.
No previous medical events or arrests per spouse's recounting.
Full documentation and protocol adherence will be completed and submitted.
Confirmed absence of respiratory and cardiac function at end of care delivery.
No recent illnesses or cardiovascular symptoms presented prior to incident.
Timelines match with spouse's account of health around 11 AM.
Physician order for cease of resuscitation followed precisely at 14:44.
